title: Des gens utilisent l'IA au lieu d'un avocat
gist: More and more people are using AI chatbots instead of lawyers to fight their own court cases — a trend called “vibe lawyering.” The problem is the AI often invents fake legal cases, which gets their claims thrown out and can cost them money. Used carefully it can help, but for now human lawyers still matter.

body.fr: Beaucoup de gens {{utilisent|use (from utiliser)}} l'{{intelligence artificielle (l'IA)|artificial intelligence (AI)}} pour {{aller au tribunal|go to court}}.

bd.meaning: Lots of people use AI to go to court.
chunks: lots of people (beaucoup de = a lot of)
use — the -ent ending marks a plural subject
artificial intelligence — note the adjective comes AFTER the noun
to go / in order to go
bd.key: pour + plain verb = “in order to.” The verb after pour stays plain (aller, not conjugated).

body.fr: Ils n'ont pas d'{{avocat|lawyer}}.

bd.meaning: They don't have a lawyer.
chunks: don't have — the ne…pas sandwich around the verb 'ont'; ne → n' before a vowel
a lawyer (un → de after a negative; de → d')
bd.key: Negation sandwich: ne … pas wraps the verb, and after it un avocat becomes d'avocat.

body.fr: C'est {{moins cher|cheaper}}.

bd.meaning: It's cheaper.
chunks: it is / that's — the set phrase c'est
bd.key: moins + adjective = “less ___.” The everyday way to say “cheaper” without a special word.

body.fr: {{On appelle ça|it's called / people call it}} le « vibe lawyering ».

bd.meaning: This is called “vibe lawyering.”
chunks: one calls / people call (on = the general 'you / people / we')
kept in English — there's no settled French term
bd.key: “On” is the workhorse for “people / you / we” in general. On appelle ça… = “this is called…”

body.fr: Mais il y a un problème.

bd.meaning: But there is a problem.
chunks: there is / there are — a fixed phrase
bd.key: il y a = “there is / are.” A set phrase — don't read it literally as “he has there.”

body.fr: L'IA {{invente|invents, makes up}} parfois des {{choses fausses|false things}}.

bd.meaning: AI sometimes invents false things.
chunks: false things — 'fausses' comes after the noun
bd.key: Adjective after the noun, and agreeing with it: choses is feminine plural, so faux becomes fausses.

body.fr: Elle invente des {{affaires de justice|court cases}} {{qui n'existent pas|that don't exist}}.

bd.meaning: It invents court cases that don't exist.
chunks: it invents ('elle' stands in for l'IA, which is feminine)
that don't exist (qui = that/which; then the ne…pas sandwich again)
bd.key: “qui” hooks a description onto a noun (“cases THAT don't exist”), and inside it the ne … pas sandwich shows up once more.

body.fr: Par exemple, Omar Rafique a un restaurant.

bd.meaning: For example, Omar Rafique has a restaurant.
bd.key: “a” (from avoir) = “has.” A tiny word that does a lot — you'll meet it again as the past helper.

body.fr: Il a un problème avec les {{impôts|taxes}}.

bd.meaning: He has a problem with taxes.
chunks: with taxes (les = 'the', where English drops it)
bd.key: French keeps “the” where English drops it: les impôts = just “taxes.”

body.fr: Il va au tribunal, mais ses documents sont {{faux|fake, false}}.

bd.meaning: He goes to court, but his documents are fake.
bd.key: “ses” = his OR her. French doesn't mark the owner's gender — context tells you which.

body.fr: Un chatbot IA {{a inventé|invented (past tense)}} ces documents.

bd.meaning: An AI chatbot invented these documents.
bd.key: Past = avoir + past form: a inventé = “invented.” The a is the same little word as “has.”

body.fr: Le {{juge|judge}} {{refuse|rejects, refuses}} son affaire.

bd.meaning: The judge rejects his case.
bd.key: “son affaire” = his/her case — again, son doesn't reveal the owner's gender.

body.fr: Dans {{plusieurs pays|several countries}}, {{de plus en plus de gens|more and more people}} vont au tribunal {{sans|without}} avocat.

bd.meaning: In several countries, more and more people go to court without a lawyer.
chunks: in several countries (pays is spelled the same singular and plural)
de plus en plus de gens
more and more people — a ready-made phrase
without a lawyer — note: no 'un' after sans
bd.key: “de plus en plus de ___” = “more and more ___.” And sans avocat drops the article — “without lawyer.”

body.fr: L'IA dit souvent aux gens : « Allez au tribunal ! Vous {{allez gagner|are going to win}} ! »

bd.meaning: AI often tells people: “Go to court! You're going to win!”
bd.key: Vous allez gagner = the aller + plain verb future. Only allez changes; gagner stays plain.

body.fr: Mais {{ce n'est pas toujours vrai|it's not always true}}.

bd.meaning: But it's not always true.
chunks: it is not (c'est with the ne…pas sandwich)
bd.key: “c'est” negates to “ce n'est pas” — the same ne … pas sandwich, wrapped around est.

body.fr: Utiliser l'IA peut {{coûter cher|be expensive (cost a lot)}}.

bd.meaning: Using AI can be expensive.
chunks: using AI / to use AI (plain verb used as 'using')
bd.key: peut coûter = “can cost” — pouvoir + plain verb, the helper-plus-plain-verb shape. Only peut conjugates.

body.fr: Une {{étudiante|student (female)}} {{doit payer|has to pay}} 7 000 dollars, parce qu'elle a {{mal utilisé|used badly}} l'IA.

bd.meaning: A student has to pay 7,000 dollars, because she used AI badly.
chunks: a (female) student — the -e ending marks her as female
because she (que → qu' before a vowel)
used badly (a…utilisé = past; mal sits in the middle)
bd.key: doit payer = “has to pay” (devoir + plain verb). And the -e on étudiante is what tells you the student is a woman.

body.fr: {{Même|even}} les {{vrais avocats|real lawyers}} font des {{erreurs|mistakes}} avec l'IA.

bd.meaning: Even real lawyers make mistakes with AI.
chunks: real lawyers — 'vrais' comes BEFORE the noun
bd.key: “vrais” goes BEFORE the noun. A handful of everyday adjectives (vrai, bon, grand, petit) break the usual “after the noun” rule.

body.fr: Mais l'IA peut aussi {{aider|help}}.

bd.meaning: But AI can also help.
bd.key: Again pouvoir + plain verb: peut aider = “can help.”

body.fr: À Londres, une femme utilise une IA et {{récupère|gets back, recovers}} son argent.

bd.meaning: In London, a woman uses an AI and gets her money back.
chunks: gets her money back (récupérer = get back/recover)
bd.key: “à” before a city = “in”: à Londres = “in London.”

body.fr: Mais un vrai avocat {{parle pour elle|speaks for her}} au tribunal.

bd.meaning: But a real lawyer speaks for her in court.
chunks: for her (elle = her, after the little word 'pour')
bd.key: After a little word like “pour,” “she” becomes “elle”: pour elle = “for her.”

body.fr: {{Pour le moment|for now}}, les avocats humains {{restent|remain, stay}} importants.

bd.meaning: For now, human lawyers remain important.
chunks: for now / for the moment — a fixed phrase
human lawyers ('humains' after the noun, plural)
bd.key: Adjectives echo the noun's number: both humains and importants take a plural -s to match “avocats.”

grammar.en: aller + plain verb = the near future. Only aller changes with the subject; the second verb (gagner) stays plain. Your “future = aller + verb” key.
avoir + past participle = the past (passé composé). avoir changes with the subject (a) and the main verb takes its past form. Your “past = avoir/être + verb” key.
the ne … pas sandwich. It wraps the verb, and it turns up all over this story: n'ont pas, qui n'existent pas, ce n'est pas.

quiz.q: Pourquoi le juge refuse l'affaire d'Omar Rafique ?
quiz.a: Parce que ses documents sont faux — l'IA les a inventés. (Because his documents are fake — the AI made them up.)

flag: Two honesty flags: “vibe lawyering” stays in English — there's no settled French term, and a French writer would likely do the same. And affaires de justice qui n'existent pas is clear and correct, but a native might say décisions qui n'existent pas (“rulings that don't exist”); I chose affaires as the higher-frequency word for you.
